data.title: Gold And Silver: The Relevance Of Copper
data.description:
Gold and silver: why I remain invested

Readers following our writings know that we have been long-term investors in gold and silver. Indeed, the outsized price rallies in both metals have helped to widen our portfolios’ lead over the broader market (and I will detail our gold and silver exposure at the end of the article). To wit, the chart below illustrates the price performance of the iShares Silver Trust (SLV) and SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) ETFs YTD. As seen, the SLV price almost doubled (an increase of 96.13%), and GLD prices rallied almost 60%, far exceeding the already robust YTD return of the SP500’s return of 16.63% or the tech sector’s return of 21.67%.

With gold and silver prices hovering around multi-year peaks, we also begin to see increasing anxiety and confusion among our readers. As such, in the rest of this article, I will explain why I keep maintaining a sizable exposure toward these precious metals (especially toward silver). Among the many factors behind my decision, I will concentrate on 2 of them in particular in the rest of this article: the inventory data and the production outlook.

Inventory data

From the last data that I’ve reviewed, inventories of silver in major markets like London, New York, and Shanghai are all at multi-year lows. As an example, the next chart (provided by Bloomberg) tracks the exchange-tracked silver inventories at the Shanghai Gold Exchange and the Shanghai Futures Exchange in the past 10 years, from 2015 through November 21, 2025. Both stockpiles show a similar trend, peaking around the start of 2021 before entering a sustained decline. The Shanghai Gold Exchange (white line) inventory, after reaching a high of nearly 6,000 tons in early 2021, has slumped to its lowest level in a decade as of November 21, 2025. The Shanghai Futures Exchange (blue line) followed a very similar pattern and also indicates a severe contraction in China's silver stockpiles since 2021.

Similarly, as you can see from the second chart below, COMEX data also show a sharp decline of its silver inventory in 2025. The reading started at around 500M oz in early 2025 and peaked at 531M oz in late December. It has since dropped from this peak to the current level of around 456M oz.

Next, I will explain why the low inventories are likely to further decrease given the production-demand deficit.

Relevance of copper production

The supply-demand dynamic for silver has been very tight for years. As a reflection, the following 2025 forecast for supply and demand is provided by the Silver Institute. The forecasts point to a persisting deficit in silver supply, as seen. More specifically, total global silver supply is projected to grow marginally by 2% to 1,030.6 million oz in 2025. In comparison, the demand side of the equation is anticipated to be around 1,148 million oz, resulting in a deficit of -118.2 million oz.

The picture for gold demand and supply is quite similar in my view. As an example, the following statistics were provided by the World Gold Council. The data are as of Q2 2025 and represent the organization’s latest update. As seen, total global gold supply dialed in at 1,248.8 tons for Q2 2025, tightly balanced by a demand of 1,248.8 tons to the dot. Looking ahead, a few changes could further tighten or even widen the supply-demand tension. Macroeconomic uncertainties (such as global trade policy and geopolitical turbulence) could be a key support to sustaining the momentum of gold and silver prices. These uncertainties have been detailed in my recent articles, and I won’t further elaborate anymore here.

Here, I will focus on a more recent development: the production cut from China’s top copper smelters. The following quotes provide more details of this development:

Reuters news (Nov 28, 2025) China’s top copper smelters will cut production by over 10% in 2026, to counter overcapacity in the industry that has led to increasingly distorted copper concentrate processing fees, according to a Chinese market information provider… The China Smelters Purchase Team (CSPT), a group of the top copper smelters, said its members agreed to cut production to improve market conditions, according to Shanghai Metals Market on Friday.

For readers unfamiliar with the mining and smelting industry, silver and gold are recovered as byproducts of copper processing to a large degree.

Based on my rough estimate (the keyword being rough), the 10% reduction mentioned above for China’s top copper smelters could cause a decrease of approximately 3% of global mined silver, widening the silver deficit mentioned earlier. The impact on gold production will be smaller. But given the very tight dynamics of the current gold supply and demand, a small production decrease could disturb the balance.

Other risks and final thoughts

My above projections could go wrong in several places. The top reason is recycling. If you recall from the two charts I showed above, recycled silver and gold represent a sizable portion of the global supply. For example, in Q2 2025, recycled gold contributed 347.2 tons to the global supply, more than 1/3 of the gold produced by mine production (908.6 tons). The potential for recycled gold and silver is difficult to estimate. From our experiences, higher gold and silver prices seem to be always able to motivate more investors to recycle. The next two wildcards are the AI supercycle and also the potential impact of US tariffs. All metals mentioned in this article (gold, silver, and copper) find wide applications in digital and AI technologies and are traded between the US and its major trading partners. As such, unfavorable developments in AI infrastructure investments and/or tariffs could be a downside risk to my analysis above.

To conclude, the goal for this article is to explain why I keep maintaining a sizable allocation towards gold and silver. Despite the outsize price rally in recent years, I see several catalysts to support such exposure. Besides the macroeconomic factors detailed in my earlier articles, two recent developments (the low stockpile and the copper production cut) provide two more catalysts, at least for the near term. Finally, as promised earlier, the last chart below shows my silver and gold allocation in our growth portfolio. As seen, I am allocating 7% towards these precious metals, with the majority of the exposure on silver. For our withdrawal/survival portfolio, I am allocating all our precious metal exposure to gold.

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor: I Don't Think Growth Is About To End
data.description:
My last article on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) was right after DeepSeek day. My perception was that the selloff was just creating a buying opportunity. Since then, the stock has reached new heights and has risen more than 40%. In the meantime, it has become clear not only that the market reaction at that time was not the most appropriate, but also that in recent quarters, TSM has proven to be one of the companies that has benefited most from the momentum.

As TSM stock has risen sharply, it is normal to have some apprehension or discomfort about remaining optimistic, but looking at the valuation, there is a premium that is not that large, but for a company that is in a very premium momentum, which ends up balancing this game and supporting a continuation of a buy rating.

TSMC's Risks

In the Bears and Bulls summary on the stock's main page, you can get a good overview of what TSMC's case is. As the three highlighted bulls mentioned, TSMC is a company that benefits greatly from AI demand and has good prospects due to both internal factors and secular trends. On the other hand, there are risks and uncertainties, such as cyclicality and geopolitical risks.

Starting with geopolitical risk, although I do not believe it is a risk that can be completely overlooked, given the magnitude it would have if tensions were to arise in Taiwan, the degree of “disaster” being so high is also what makes me feel a little less concerned. What I mean is that I don't believe there is a high probability of a Chinese invasion of Taiwan, for a number of reasons, both because of the magnitude it could have (with an escalation and potential disagreements with other countries around the world) and because of China's culture of being patient and waiting decades, perhaps centuries, for the “best moment” to do something without war. On top of that, even if something did happen, I believe that TSMC shareholders would have more to worry about than just a drawdown in the stock.

As for the risk of cyclicality, this is, in my view, the main negative point in TSMC's case. As I will discuss in the next section, I am very optimistic about the company's future, both in the short and long term, but at the same time, the medium term is unknown. Today, I don't even need to mention that the momentum for TSMC is excellent, right? Demand for its products is red-hot, even exceeding production capacity at some levels. As mentioned on the earnings call, TSMC is working to increase production capacity and try to close this gap:

Talking about the CoWoS capacity, all I can say is continue the 3 months ago, we are working very hard to narrow the gap between the demand and supply. We are still working to increase the capacity in 2026. The real number, we probably update you next year. Today, all I want to say about the AI everything related like front-end and back-end capacity is very tight. We are working very hard to make sure that the gap will be narrow, but all I can say is we are working very hard.

Even though I believe this is very positive for TSMC, we need to be a little cautious. This moment will not last forever. Now, all companies want to manufacture their AI chips, and TSMC, which has the key partnerships and technology to do so, benefits greatly. But if companies like Amazon (AMZN) or Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) eventually reduce the intensity of their R&D for developing new chips, as well as when/if companies slow down their purchases of GPUs from Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and Nvidia (NVDA), TSMC will be affected. This does not seem to be a concern right now, but it is a concern that will eventually arise. It may not be in one or even five years, but it seems inevitable to me.

And when we talk about this, it is important to note that the company is positioning itself to capture growth, that is, it is investing in R&D, investing in CapEx, and, in addition, its margin is at the top. Even if revenue does not slow down that much, we could see a compression in shareholder value in worse cycles, as the chart below suggests.

TSMC: A Unique Company With Unique Long-Term Trends

One of my favorite charts for TSMC is the one below.

In the 1980s, chips were 3μm, which compared to 2nm chips may seem large and obsolete. But 3μm is equivalent to 0.00011 inches. 2nm is so small that it is even difficult to conceive, since the calculation to represent this is: 7.874e-8 inches.

In my view, this not only proves TSMC's brilliant execution over the past decades, but also supports our belief that it will also be the leader in the coming decades, such as in 1nm, which should come in mid-2030, and consequently, continue to be the main partner of several important companies. This also supports more solid revenue growth, but for that, we need to look at longer cycles, since, as mentioned, in a few years this demand may stagnate, fall, and the margin may also be worse, but in the long term this should be sustained in a positive way.

For the next five years, estimates for this market are very good. For example, studies point to a CAGR of ~10% through 2030. But for the AI chip market, for example, the outlook is already closer to 30%. And what is happening in the market today points to this. Companies like Alphabet are developing TPUs, investing heavily in this, and this also involves TSMC. Amazon is also developing its own chips. Not to mention Nvidia itself, which in several quarters has had constrained capacity while demand was very high. Again, it is important to think about cycles, but the signs are that this cycle is not likely to end for now, especially with so much money being injected into this type of investment and with companies like Nvidia launching more powerful GPUs and scaling them up so that their customers can achieve increasingly ambitious goals related to AI, production capacity, efficiencies, and the like.

With that in mind, I believe the ideal approach is to look at the valuation in relation to TSMC's growth prospects. Today, the forward P/E is 28x, and the 5-year average is 22.6x, which is a 27% premium against its own history. Other multiples compared to this period are also higher, some 12% and some 25%. But two things need to be kept in mind about TSMC's growth.

The first is that over the last 10 years, revenue has grown at a CAGR of 15%, while EPS has grown at 18%. Looking ahead to 2026, we are talking about revenue that is expected to grow by ~20% and EPS following suit, with TSMC maintaining a high margin.

With that in mind, I ask you. Is a 25% premium over its recent history that high? Since growth is satisfactory and several trends indicate that this should continue? Is 28x earnings too much for such a unique company with wide moats that should maintain rapid growth in the short and long term? Well, in my view, no. I believe TSMC's valuation is still quite reasonable.

The Bottom Line

In short, even with some uncertainties related to the AI cycle bothering me, I believe that TSMC's investment case remains very solid, supported both by micro reasons, such as the company's moats, solid partnerships, and great financials, but also by external reasons related to the secular demand for its products.

Therefore, I maintain a buy rating for TSMC.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Acadia Healthcare: Structural Problems, Not A Temporary Rough Patch
data.description:
Company Overview

This is my first time covering Acadia Healthcare Company (ACHC), so I’m going to take things step by step.

What do they do?

Acadia is a for-profit provider of behavioral healthcare services that operates nationwide (think inpatient psychiatric hospitals, specialty treatment centers, and opioid treatment clinics). We’re talking ~250 facilities, ~11,000 beds, and ~70,000 patients treated daily. Revenue comes from government payers, with approximately 70% from Medicaid and Medicare.

The article that follows reveals that many of Acadia's cracks are foundational, rather than cyclical issues that can be patched over with a few better quarters of volume, pricing, or cost cuts.

Financial Performance

In 2024, per Seeking Alpha, Acadia generated $3.154 billion in revenue (up ~7.7% Y/Y) with a gross profit of $1.35 billion (42.81% of revenue). After accounting for SG&A, D&A, and other operating expenses, its FY24 operating income was $522.3 million. Net income was $255.6 million.

A point of contention that I’ll touch on later is Acadia’s “growth at all costs” strategy. This is clearly reflected in a large rise in capital expenditures, or capex.

Acadia’s capex was $690.4 million in FY24, falling well short of its "cash from operations" of $129.7 million. Management’s long-term goal calls for 7-9% annual revenue growth from 2026 onward.

Fast forward to “today,” both growth and margins have compressed. Q3 revenue of $851.6 million represented just a 4.4% Y/Y increase. Meanwhile, adjusted EBITDA fell from $194.3 million in the prior year to $173 million in Q3 2025. Despite this, Acadia continues to expand, adding 429 beds in Q3, including 346 to new facilities.

Its implied Q4 2025 guidance (using 9M actuals plus the latest full-year ranges) calls for ~$789-$809 million, with adjusted EBITDA between $91.9 million and $101.9 million. The company attributes the lowered Q4 guidance to volume softness, rate pressure, elevated bad debt and denials, and incremental Professional Liability and General Liability (PL/GL). Q4 is framed as not a “new baseline” for 2026.

Recent Developments

So, what is going on?

Acadia’s core growth engine has been adding high-acuity beds into Medicaid-heavy markets. Notably, Medicaid behavior has become more stringent in acute lines. There are more denials and greater pressure to shorten lengths of stay, or LOS. Moreover, federal policy changes, like a planned step-down in state-directed payments starting in 2028, are expected to complicate reimbursement further.

While all of this is going on, Acadia has faced multiple lawsuits (resulting in settlements) in the past. Right now, Acadia is the subject of ongoing government investigations.

Altogether, the incremental economic value of new beds appears to be compressing. In other words, each additional unit of volume brings a relatively lower incremental margin once PL/GL, compliance, and Medicaid rate/LOS pressures are fully reflected.

Regarding PL/GL, Acadia issued an update earlier this month regarding its FY25 PL/GL expense.

Given Acadia’s valuation of just ~$1.3 billion after its stock has fallen ~65% YTD, activist investors have become central actors.

Engine Capital and Khrom Capital have taken small stakes and, essentially, recommend an immediate halt to growth capex and a sale of assets (i.e., isolated facilities, sale-leasebacks).

In recent calls, management has softened its "growth at all costs" tone and plans to cut capex by $300 million next year. The company also closed five facilities in 2025. While this should help margins, it effectively admits that some prior growth decisions were value-destroying. Although management pleads for investors not to extrapolate Q4 headwinds into 2026, a few issues remain deeply embedded.

  • The structural increase in PL/GL expense
  • The broader liability and regulatory scrutiny
  • The Medicaid-heavy payer mix and policy changes (shorter LOS, lower revenue per patient day, and so on)

Valuation

Acadia's conservative valuation has to be viewed in a broader context.

For instance, while its TTM P/E GAAP (12.41) trades at a ~53% discount to its peers in the Health Care sector, ACHC features relatively poorer growth (e.g., Y/Y EBITDA growth of -9.06% versus +9.66% sector) and profitability (e.g., levered FCF margin of -11.07% versus 3.99% sector).

On top of this, it has risks like structurally higher PL/GL expense, ongoing government investigations, and a Medicaid-heavy payer mix facing tighter utilization and policy risk.

Final Words

Acadia is no longer just a “grow the bed base and earnings will follow” story. It is a Medicaid-heavy operator with rising legal and insurance costs, tighter utilization management, and a recent history of poor capex decisions. While management projects a 2026 rebound in margins and free cash flow, I am cautious.

The story from here hinges on whether management and the board truly pivot from asset growth to return on capital. Activist pressure has helped, but management appears to be taking incremental steps, possibly hoping for a 2026 rebound before becoming serious about restructuring.

That said, and this is an important point: the core issues remain embedded in the model. PL/GL and compliance costs are structurally higher. Medicaid and managed care are exerting more control over LOS and revenue per patient day. And so on. Subsequently, Acadia is looking more like an open-ended liability and capital allocation story than a derisked behavioral health compounder, meriting a cautious "Hold" rating.

I would like to first see clear evidence of a turnaround before jumping aboard (e.g., margin stabilization, evidence that payer pressure is manageable, prudent capital allocation, and a clean narrative on liability and culture).

data.title: Autodesk: Incredible Moat And Great Opportunities, But Too Expensive
data.description:
Thesis

Autodesk (ADSK) is a software leader with a high moat and a dominant market share. Its high switching costs, strengthening cloud ecosystem, and large AI implementation potential will continue to drive its double-digit organic growth. A new direct-to-consumer transaction model will expand its pricing power and allow Autodesk to better understand its customers. Rising net revenue retention, expanding margins, and strong fee-cash-flow generation aided by low-cost debt will allow Autodesk to continue compounding. Its valuation, however, is too high to generate a high return on this stock. I see a great business but not a great stock here and rate ADSK a Hold.

Business Description

Autodesk is a software company that produces software used in industries such as architecture, design, engineering, and construction. Computer-aided-design (CAD) software such as AutoCAD is considered essential in professions like product management, architecture, and engineering.

Autodesk classifies its products into 5 accounting product families. I say accounting because many products and services are intertwined between multiple segments, such as AutoCAD:

  • Architecture, Engineering, Construction & Operations (AECO): Software for the design, construction, and operation of buildings and infrastructure. It is Autodesk's largest product family by revenue and includes products such as

    • AEC collection: A bundle of core AEC apps such as Revit, Civil 3D, and AutoCAD.

    • Autodesk Build: Cloud-based platform that connects project teams.

    • BIM Collaborate Pro: Cloud-based design and coordination between different professionals.

  • AutoCAD & LT: Autodesk's foundational CAD product.

  • Manufacturing (MFG): Products aimed at product design and manufacturing workflows. Products include:

    • Fusion: Platform combining design, simulation, and manufacturing.

    • Inventor: 3E mechanical design software for machinery and equipment manufacturers.

    • Vault: Data management software for engineering documents.

  • Media & Entertainment (M&E): Product for digital content creators including:

    • Maya: 3D modeling, animation, and rendering software.

    • 3ds Max: For design visualization and animation.

  • Other: Legacy/emerging tools.

You can check out their official product documentation page to see how many products Autodesk actually has. I will later explain Autodesk's growth rates for Q3 FY2026. You also do not need to understand which products belong to which product family, as it is a whole complex ecosystem. I think the most important takeaway from its products is that they are essential to professionals.

They are so essential as it allows multiple professionals to work on the creation, design, and modification of a project. According to 6sense.com, AutoCAD has the highest market share at 40%, and Autodesk, AutoCAD Mechanical, and Autodesk Revit all rank 3rd, 4th, and 5th. In fact, ADSK’s CAD software has around 65% of the market share, essentially making it a monopoly.

The fact that it has an essential product that companies will keep using due to the high switching costs and students will keep learning it to get hired creates a powerful moat. This advantage provides two opportunities. Firstly, ADSK has the advantage of collecting more data from users to improve its products and develop a whole ecosystem with its software. This has already happened with its cloud services (Autodesk Construction Cloud) and other products that allow many professionals to work on the same project. And, as projects become more complex and require other professionals from different firms, it will cause a network effect where everyone must be using the same ecosystem. These effects are already starting to happen, as Andrew Anagnost, ADSK's CEO, mentioned other industries such as biopharma, aerospace, energy, transportation, automotive, and education already using Autodesk’s products.

New Products and Services

One important breakthrough that has shown high adoption rates is its AI features, which, in the words of the CEO,

“Autodesk AI will move beyond traditional, deterministic and rule-based parametric CAD kernels to deliver adaptive and context aware, AI-driven CAD engines. These engines will dramatically expand what's possible across the entire project life cycle, while eliminating much of the repetitive work and rework that slows projects down today."

Its AI-powered Sketch AutoConstrain in Fusion is a tool that automatically applies geometric and dimensional constraints to a sketch, reducing the need to do it manually. Since its launch this year, users have used this feature in 60% of cases, with 90% of those sketches being fully constrained. It is common to see industries attempting to use AI in their workflow and implementing it into their services without seeing real gains in productivity or in value to the user. High adoption rates of its AI features give me confidence that additional AI features can provide actual value to the user in the form of productivity gains. Overall, these high adoption rates place Autodesk at an advantage, as the higher the usage, the better the AI products can be improved. I see many applications of AI in its CADs due to the repetitive workflow of this kind of software. The automatization of certain processes will continue to expand across its whole ecosystem, and whole projects created by an AI using prompts are not a future that is that far out.

New Transaction Model

In Q4 FY 2024, ADSK’s management announced the adoption of a new transaction model where it essentially eliminated the distributors of its licenses and allowed it to have a more direct relationship with its customers.

I see many benefits of having a direct relationship with its customers, the most important being that it can collect data on the purchasing behavior of its clients for optimized pricing and demand predictability. The new transaction model also mechanically improves its revenue, as reseller compensation will no longer be deducted from gross revenue but from gross profit. As mentioned on its Q3 FY 2026 earnings call, this has shown effects as its YoY revenue growth was 18%, with 6% attributed to mechanical growth and 12% organic.

Q3 FY26 Results

Autodesk beat top-line and bottom-line expectations and showed increased revenue growth rates across most of its product families. As mentioned before, it is misleading to base growth off revenue growth, as the new transaction model mechanically increases it. However, it has shown a net income and operating income YoY growth of 24% and 33.3%, respectively.

In terms of user growth, in its latest earnings, it referenced a net revenue retention above 110%, meaning that existing customers spent 10% more YoY (more seats, additional products/services, or both). A retention above 100% is very good news for any software, as it means that Autodesk has purchasing power and a scalable product. Together with its growth rates, its retention rate above 100% for the past couple of quarters signals a developing ecosystem.

Another important feature for an ecosystem is convergence across its products. It looks like convergence across many professionals for the same project is already happening, as Andrew Anagnost said this on the Q3 FY26 earnings call:

“Our customers in AECO, architecture, engineering, construction and operations, are demanding convergence to reduce risk, increase quality and optimize costs and resource use during the design and build phase of an asset and to yield enhanced efficiency, resilience and reuse during the operations and maintenance phase of an asset. Autodesk Construction Cloud has growing momentum with owners, designers, GCs and subcontractors seeking to converge design and construction workflows.”- Andrew Anagnost, CEO.

It looks like efficiency improvements, as projects become complex, are the main driver of demand. Efficiency improvements not only come from the convergence of its software but also from AI implementation. On its earnings call, Andrew Anagnost also explained its strategy with AI implementation:

“We're going to be kind of moving along with our customers here and focusing on key areas of adoption and finding levers of productivity that make a real impact on them.

We're starting with tasks, AutoConstrain is a classy example of a task within the modeling. We're going to do a lot of that. That task automation is highly protective of our existing business.”- Andrew Anagnost, CEO.

As I have said before, effective AI implementation requires large amounts of data and a product that can genuinely be improved by AI. Autodesk’s products have both, and a high demand for efficiency improvement through convergence will only accelerate the implementation of AI.

With a product with a high moat and a developing ecosystem, AI is the cherry on top, as it means that Autodesk has the flexibility of charging a higher price for its AI services in the years ahead. Even if its competition implements AI without any charge, high switching costs will not make it economically viable for most users to switch. The efficiency improvements have already been shown, and these features are the worst they will ever be.

Commentary on Financials

Being that it is a software company with a high moat and has a large market share, it is the most profitable among its competitors with an ROA, ROIC, and ROE of 11%, 27%, and 50%, respectively. In fact, it is the most profitable it has been since it became profitable in 2020, excluding ROE, as leverage has consistently been decreasing.

Its non-GAAP gross margin, EBIT margin, and profit margin are 93%, 36%, and 18%, respectively. These high margins allow it the flexibility to pursue other ventures and incur additional costs without affecting its bottom line. I decided to use non-GAAP measures, as these exclude non-cash expenses, which more accurately reflect its operations.

Deleveraging

I think it is important to talk about Autodesk's deleveraging, as it has the massive advantage of securing low interest rates on its debt. By 2021 ADSK had finished its transition from selling perpetual software licenses to subscription models. This allowed it to generate more than $1B in annual free cash flow while new debt was not acquired, meaning that cash grew faster than debt. I think this trend will continue moving forward due to how secure its cash flows are, which can be reinvested at high profitability rates or distributed.

Valuation

I expect to see a higher revenue growth rate in the next year or two due to its new transaction model mechanically increasing its revenue on top of its organic growth, which I expect to be between 12% and 13%. In its Q3 FY 2026 earnings call, management said it expects its FY 2026 non-GAAP operating margin to be 40%, which I assume over the next 5 years. Before showing the results, it is also worth noting that its WACC is essentially its cost of equity calculated using the CAPM model, as it has such low amounts of debt. I think a realistic assumption for its sensitivity analysis is that ADSK will take on additional debt, causing the DCF share price and sensitivity analysis results to differ.

The intrinsic price of $324.7 indicates a potential upside of 6%. This, along with its high valuation metrics, is the reason why I give it a Hold rating.

Risks

I think there is little downside to this stock due to the moat of its products, but I do see some issues that deter me from giving Autodesk a buy rating. The main issue with my thesis is that I see no catalyst in its products, as only professionals use it. The global CAD software market is projected to grow from 12.4B in 2025 to 19.1B by 2032, showing a CAGR of 6.4%. This is not a great growth rate, and if my thesis is correct, then it may be possible for an additional 1-2%. Its current valuation metrics are not great, with a P/E ratio of 60 and a PEG of 24. With the DCF showing an upside of 7%, there is not a clear mispricing by the market.

Another risk I see with my thesis regarding AI is that, because of the repetitive nature of switching CAD software, AI also has the potential to significantly reduce switching costs. This can actually reduce Autodesk's pricing power and increase costs to differentiate its products.

Conclusion

ADSK’s moat and large market share give it the opportunity to create an ecosystem that enhances a network effect. Its software has proven to be AI scalable, and I see great opportunities in this area, which will further enhance its already high profitability. This gives it the flexibility to take on other investment ventures and/or distribute its earnings. Despite its large opportunities, its current valuation does not show a large upside potential, with the DCF showing a margin of safety of 6%. I also do not see a large downside potential due to the predictive nature of its cash flows. I see this stock as a safe bet fit for individuals with a long-term horizon and a low risk tolerance. Therefore, I rate this stock a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Birkenstock: Customers Walking On Sunshine, The Stock On Thin Ice - Hold
data.description:
Intro

Birkenstock Holding plc (BIRK) has its roots back into 1774 when Johannes Birkenstock registered as a shoemaker in a small town in the middle of Germany. Two centuries later, Carl Birkenstock launched the first contoured footbed open sandal that would eventually conquer the world.

Fast-forward to today: the company is still in business, but meanwhile publicly listed in New York. The October 2023 IPO took place at $46 per share. After a short-lived run to above $60, today the stock is trading where it started.

As Birkenstock as a company has been growing strongly, I am taking a look at the stock, evaluating the last results and giving an outlook to the upcoming full-year earnings, set to be released in a few weeks.

Note: BIRK stock trades in USD on the NYSE, while accounting is in EUR. It was a choice for the U.S. market for deeper liquidity, a broader investor base, and the simple fact that America now accounts for roughly half of total sales.

Will BIRK Stumble?

Birkenstock reported solid third-quarter results (presentation here), which, however, modestly missed some estimates. They weren’t bad. I was not able to spot a negative surprise, but a few analysts seemed to have expected more.

Anyway, revenue in Q3 of the fiscal year came in at €635 million and grew by a healthy 12% (16% in constant currency). Gross margins expanded 100 basis points to 60.5%, while adjusted EBITDA expanded at a faster pace with +17%, reaching €218 million with a margin of 34.4% (+140 bps). Adjusted EPS was €0.62 or 27% higher compared to the previous year. Thanks to higher EBITDA, leverage fell modestly.

Until here, nothing to get uncomfortable about.

Digging a bit deeper, we can see that every region grew solidly in the double digits, with APAC showing the strongest dynamic. B2B channels expanded faster than self-owned DTC (+15% vs. +9%).

DTC (direct to consumer) is usually a higher-margin business, as wholesale partners want to get their cut in B2B. Birkenstock was able to increase margins, nonetheless, thanks to better production capacity utilization and, first and foremost, higher realized prices (mix of price adjustments and low promo activity).

This does not apply solely to gross, but also to operating EBITDA margins where price adjustments were the main driver. Not framed and next to prices, you can see the efficiency gains from capacity absorption.

As mentioned briefly in the intro, the Americas generate about half of total sales.

On the call, management explained that capacity expansion progressed ahead of plan, with new facilities in Germany and India contributing to both volume growth and unit-cost improvements (benefiting margins as shown above).

It is important to understand, Birkenstock does not see itself as a sandal or some casual shoe company. It sees itself in the wellness and luxury sector. Accordingly, management confirmed its pricing strategy, adjusting prices to higher tariff costs, with no promotional activity. Continued ASP (average selling price) increases across all channels proved that customers have been willing to pay the premium for Birkenstocks.

So far, I should say, as I am not fully convinced this will be possible down the road without casualties.

Regarding the outlook, Birkenstock guided for fiscal 2025 (BIRK will report in two weeks) revenue to grow at the high-end of its initial guidance range of 15-17% in constant currency (reported figures likely a bit lower). Additionally, BIRK forecasted adj EBITDA margin in a range of 31.3-31.8%, despite the strong depreciation of the US Dollar and tariffs.

So far, so good. The thing, and my main issue, is that these figures were from summer. August to be more precise. That’s a full four months in the past. A lot has happened since then, especially on the consumer front. Many clothing, apparel, and shoe companies (but also other consumer-dependent companies) have reported about cautious consumers.

On the last call, management already acknowledged a more cautious consumer backdrop globally but expressed confidence in the brand’s resilience, its pricing power, the ability to prevent tariffs from eating into margins, and a bit more into the future long-term structural growth drivers.

If Birkenstock manages to practically ignore consumer trends, posting ongoing solid growth rates and operational efficiency gains, the stock might see higher prices. However, if growth decelerates, I fear the stock could make new lows since its IPO. I acknowledge that the stock is looking much more promising now than in the early days of its public existence. The P/E ratio dropped to the low 20s, which is far more realistic than the values seen before.

At 22x P/E, we need some solid, low double-digit growth rates to justify this multiple. I cannot imagine the stock keeping up if sales growth loses steam, falling into the single digits. It will be interesting to see if further efficiency gains and margin increases will have been achieved during the now-closed fourth quarter, which included the key summer season.

While operational execution remains excellent (backwards-looking) and the strategic direction clear, the valuation currently reflects an optimistic scenario with continued solid expansion.

The enterprise value is currently a bit above $9 billion. FCF over the last twelve months was, adjusted for negative working capital effects, around €360 million or $420 million. We need to convert from EUR to USD and not forget to subtract interest payments and leasing liability repayments from FCF, which under IFRS unfortunately are in the financing section of the cash flow statement (I view them as operational cash outflows). The result is an EV / FCF multiple of around 22x, which needs flawless execution.

A surprising slowdown in business activity due to cautious consumer spending carries the risk of sending the stock lower, potentially to new lows below $40. That’s why I rate BIRK a hold, wanting to assess the coming results first.

Risks To My Thesis

A sudden resurgence in consumer spending (especially a strong holiday season in the U.S. and China) could easily push revenue and margins above even the new guidance, justifying or even driving multiple expansion. Second, faster-than-expected DTC penetration with a higher share of total sales would likely accelerate the margin progression and make today’s valuation look conservative in hindsight. Finally, any meaningful acquisition (e.g., complementary wellness/lifestyle brands) could add new growth vectors the market isn’t pricing in yet.

Conclusion

Let’s wait for the coming Q4 and FY 2025 results in a couple of weeks, before we get a better view on the business dynamics.

data.title: Franklin Utilities Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: US equities delivered strong performance over the third quarter, buoyed, in part, by easing trade tensions and optimism surrounding artificial intelligence (AI). The US Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) interest-rate cut in September also supported market sentiment, as did generally robust corporate earnings. Both the S&P 500 Index and the NASDAQ Composite Index ended the quarter at new record highs, while returns for the Dow Jones Industrial Average were more muted. In this environment, growth investing surpassed value in the large-capitalization tier, while it was the reverse in the mid- and small-capitalization segments, with small-cap stocks overall performing better than their large- and mid-cap counterparts.
  • Contributors: Stock selection effects were the primary contributors to the fund’s relative performance. Stock selection in the electric utilities and independent power producers and energy traders industries were leading contributors to relative returns.
  • Detractors: Allocation effects dampened the fund’s relative results. Off-benchmark exposures to the oil and gas storage and transportation industry and the renewable electricity industry had a negative impact on relative results.
  • Outlook: We believe that power demand across the United States is likely to improve significantly, along with industry capital spending on data center development.

Performance Review

  • The fund (Advisor Class without sales charges) (FRUAX) outperformed the S&P 500 Utilities Index benchmark in the third quarter of 2025. In the electric utilities industry, Constellation Energy’s (CEG) stock appreciation lagged the benchmark, and an underweight position contributed to relative returns. The company has been benefiting from a broader resurgence in nuclear power.
  • Talen Energy (TLN)(not part of the benchmark) was a leading contributor in the independent power producers and energy traders industry. The power generation and infrastructure company announced the acquisition of two gas-fired power plants and deepened its relationship with Amazon Web Services (a subsidiary of Amazon.com (AMZN), which is not held by the fund) through a long-term power purchase agreement.
  • Relative performance was hindered by an off-benchmark position in National Grid (NGG) in the multi-utilities industry. A combination of governance failures, regulatory risk and leadership turnover impacted investor sentiment around the UK multinational utility company and weighed on its stock.

Outlook

  • We continue to believe that power demand across the United States is likely to improve significantly, along with industry capital spending on data center development. Utilities are also spending record levels of capital in transmission and distribution (T&D) to make the grid more reliable and resilient. The increasing severity of weather in the past several years is only increasing the desire to improve T&D infrastructure so that it is less impacted by storm activity, and many electric utilities are making this a top priority.
  • With more capital to spend in the industry, the importance of utilities regulation is higher than ever. Utilities need to balance the need for improved reliability with the burden that is placed on customer bills when financing their investment decisions. Finding the right balance between growing the business and keeping utility services affordable for customers is a challenge utilities face.
  • Utilities remain sensitive to long-term interest rates, and the direction of interest rates is uncertain as the Trump administration begins implementing a set of economic initiatives that differ from those of recent administrations. Dividends remain a key component of the investment opportunity for utility investors, but investment opportunities have companies directing more cash toward organic reinvestment and less toward dividends.
  • The recently passed One Big Beautiful Bill legislation has provisions that curb tax-credit benefits for solar and wind renewable development, resulting in some uncertainty about the future of new electricity supply. Given that economic growth is driving demand, policies that restrict supply could have an additional negative impact on the prices customers pay for their electricity, further exacerbating the affordability concerns at the forefront of regulatory discussions.

As of 09/30/2025 the fund’s Advisor Class shares received a 4 star and Class A shares received a 3 star overall Morningstar rating™, measuring risk-adjusted returns against 57, 56 and 50 Utilities funds over the 3-, 5- and 10- year periods, respectively. A fund’s overall rating is derived from a weighted average of the performance figures associated with its 3-, 5- and 10-year (if applicable) rating metrics.

Benchmark(s)

Benchmark 1=S&P 500 Utilities Index Benchmark 2=S&P 500 Index

Performance data quoted represents past performance, which does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the figures shown. Principal value and investment returns will fluctuate, and investors’ shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than the original cost. Performance would have been lower if fees had not been waived in various periods. Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions and the deduction of all fund expenses. Returns with sales charge reflect a deduction of the stated maximum sales charge. An investor cannot invest directly in an index, and unmanaged index returns do not reflect any fees, expenses or sales charges. Returns for periods of less than one year are not annualized. All classes of shares may not be available to all investors or through all distribution channels. For current month-end performance, please visit franklintempleton.com.

The fund began offering Advisor Class shares on 12/31/1996 and the fund began offering A Class shares on 9/10/2018. Performance quotations have been calculated as follows: (a) for Advisor Class periods prior to 12/31/1996, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A1 performance; for A Class periods prior to 9/10/2018, a restated figure is used based on the fund’s Class A1 performance. The performance was adjusted to take into account differences in class-specific operating expenses and maximum sales charges. (b) For periods after share class offering, performance for the specific share class is used, reflecting the expenses and maximum sales charges applicable to that class.

Gross expenses are the fund’s total annual operating expenses as of the fund’s prospectus available at the time of publication. Net expenses are capped under a contractual agreement, which cannot be terminated prior to 01/31/2026 without Board consent. Actual expenses may be higher and may impact portfolio returns.

What are the Risks?

All investments involve risks, including possible loss of principal. To the extent the portfolio invests in a concentration of certain securities, regions or industries, it is subject to increased volatility. Equity securities are subject to price fluctuation and possible loss of principal. Securities issued by utility companies have been historically sensitive to interest rate changes. When interest rates fall, utility securities prices, and thus a utilities fund’s share price, tend to rise; when interest rates rise, their prices generally fall. The manager may consider environmental, social and governance (ESG) criteria in the research or investment process; however, ESG considerations may not be a determinative factor in security selection. In addition, the manager may not assess every investment for ESG criteria, and not every ESG factor may be identified or evaluated. These and other risks are discussed in the fund’s prospectus.

Glossary

Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) is an unmanaged index composed of 30 blue-chip stocks, each with annual sales exceeding $7 billion. The DJIA is price-weighted, reflects large-cap companies representative of U.S. industry, and historically has moved in tandem with other major market indexes such as the S&P 500. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC.

Nasdaq Composite Index is a market-capitalization-weighted index that is designed to represent the performance of NASDAQ securities and includes over 3,000 stocks. Source: Nasdaq OMX.

Important Information

The information provided is not a complete analysis of every material fact regarding any country, market, industry, security or fund. Because market and economic conditions are subject to change, comments, opinions and analyses are rendered as of the date of this material and may change without notice. A portfolio manager’s assessment of a particular security, investment or strategy is not intended as individual investment advice or a recommendation or solicitation to buy, sell or hold any security or to adopt any investment strategy; it is intended only to provide insight into the fund’s portfolio selection process. Holdings are subject to change.

The S&P 500 Utilities Index comprises those companies included in the S&P 500 that are classi1ed as members of the GICS utilities sector. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved. The S&P 500 Index features 500 leading U.S. publicly traded companies, with a primary emphasis on market capitalization. Source: © S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC. All rights reserved.

Important data provider notices and terms available at www.franklintempletondatasources.com. All data is subject to change.

Morningstar Rating™: Source: Morningstar®, 09/30/2025. For each mutual fund and ETF with at least a 3-year history, Morningstar calculates a Morningstar Rating™ based on how a fund ranks on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure against other funds in the same category. This measure takes into account variations in a fund’s monthly performance, and does not take into account the effects of sales charges and loads, placing more emphasis on downward variations and rewarding consistent performance. The top 10% of funds in each category receive 5 stars, the next 22.5% receive 4 stars, the next 35% receive 3 stars, the next 22.5% receive 2 stars and the bottom 10% receive 1 star. The weights are: 100% 3-year rating for 36-59 months of total returns, 60% 5-year rating/40% 3-year rating for 60-119 months of total returns, and 50% 10-year rating/30% 5-year rating/20% 3-year rating for 120 or more months of total returns. While the 10-year overall star rating formula seems to give the most weight to the 10-year period, the most recent 3-year period actually has the greatest impact because it is included in all three rating periods. The fund’s Advisor Class shares received a Morningstar Rating of 4, 4 and 4 and fund’s Class A shares received a Morningstar Rating of 4, 3 and 4 star(s) for the 3-, 5- and 10-year periods, respectively. Franklin Templeton provides this fund’s Morningstar Rating™ for Advisor Class and Class A shares only. Other share classes may have different Morningstar ratings.

Franklin Distributors, LLC. Member FINRA/SIPC.

© Franklin Templeton. All rights reserved.

Before investing, carefully consider a fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. You can find this and other information in each prospectus, or summary prospectus, if available, at www.franklintempleton.com. Please read it carefully.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: How Stock Markets Might React After The Federal Reserve's December Meeting
data.description:
In the last month following the U.S. Federal Reserve’s rate policy meeting, stock markets pulled back from an all-time high. Nasdaq (QQQ) and S&P 500 (SPY) (IVV) fell to levels not seen since September. At the time, fears of circular investing in companies that supply artificial intelligence solutions fueled the fear. In that time, the Challenger report indicated an increase in job cuts year-over-year. In response, the CME Group’s FedWatch tool predicted an 87.2% probability of a 25 bps rate cut at its upcoming meeting.

Since November 24, the iShares Russell 2000 ETF (IWM) has outperformed, gaining around 9%. The small-cap index is the strongest indication that investors expect the Fed to cut rates. What will happen to stock markets after 2 p.m. on Dec. 10? What are the chances of stock markets pricing in looser conditions? Global markets already dismissed Japan’s 40-year bond yield spiking to record highs on November 21.

Investors have three things to consider.

1/ Job Cuts in November

The Bureau of Labor Statistics will post the next jobs report on December 16, the week after the Fed’s decision. In the report’s absence, readers should look at the Challenger Report. Employers announced 71,321 job cuts in November. It increased by 24% Y/Y but is down by 53% from the 153,074 cuts announced in October.

The report noted that November’s total is the highest for the month since 2022, when employers cut 76,835 jobs. Chief revenue officer for Challenger, Gray & Christmas, Andy Challenger, said that the sequential layoff plan decline is a positive sign. In November 2008 and 2022, employer cuts rose above the 70,000 level. Conversely, layoff plans tended to stay below that level near the end of the year. Corporations had a best practice of job cuts at times other than the holidays.

The Fed might assess the high job cuts in telecommunications as expected in a mature industry. Verizon Communications (VZ) announced on November 20 that it would cut over 13,000 jobs. The firm’s new CEO, Dan Shulman, said it would significantly cut outsourcing and other outside labor expenses. Verizon plans to cut its current cost structure to invest in its “customer value proposition.” Readers may infer that it would invest in AI chatbots to enhance customer experiences.

The Fed is aware that the U.S. economy might have a “K” shape, where AI is growing quickly while the rest of the economy lags. Still, the Challenger report indicated 12,377 job cuts announced in November. In 2025, the sector announced 153,536 job cuts, up 17% Y/Y.

Food companies, the service sector, retail, non-profits, media, and news sectors all announced cuts.

2/ CME FedWatch Tool

A month ago, the FedWatch tool reported a 62% chance of the target rate falling to 350-375 bps, down 25 bps. This jumped to 87.2% at the time of this article.

The U.S. dollar (DXY) (UUP) also anticipates lower rates. The dollar has declined steadily since November 23-24.

U.S. Treasury bonds (TLT) more decisively anticipated lower rates. The 20+ year treasury bill ETF broke below a $90 support zone to trade at around $88.00. The 7-10 year treasury bond (IEF) also declined, although it is currently within a trading band of $96.49-$97.50.

Investors bought shares of financial institutions at this time. Lower interest rates help the economy. This increases transaction volumes, helping bank profits. Wells Fargo (WFC), Citigroup (C), JPMorgan Chase (JPM), and Bank of America (BAC) are among the banks trading higher in the last month.

Above: BAC stock has a ‘Strong Buy’ rating from Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating. In the grades below, BAC stock scores the highest on profitability. It is also first in its industry ranking out of 31 firms.

In Canada, however, banks performed even better than their U.S. counterparts in the last month.

Even though TD Bank (TD) and Bank of Montreal (BMO) score poorly on valuation and growth, they gained 9.26% and 5.32%, respectively, in the last month. The Bank of Nova Scotia (BNS), CIBC (CM), and Royal Bank (RY) also rewarded investors.

3/ Rising Inflation

The Truflation US inflation index is 2.45%. This rose steadily since bottoming in August.

The Fed has a dual mandate of achieving maximum employment and stable prices. Although the methodology of data collection in Truflation differs, the value shown above is still above the Fed’s 2% inflation target rate. The central bank cannot satisfy both dual mandates equally.

Lowering rates risks accelerating inflation. Conversely, it stimulates the economy, encouraging companies to hire workers. The Fed will likely treat tariffs as a transitory driver of inflation. Importers and consumers bought goods in advance to avoid those duties. That drove demand higher, increasing prices.

Companies like Costco (COST) and Procter & Gamble (PG) wait for the Supreme Court to rule on the legality of tariffs. Costco is getting ahead of the ruling by suing the Administration to preserve its rights to full refunds on them.

Investors should exercise extra caution on companies in the consumer goods sector. PG stock is in a year-long decline, hovering at 2.0% above its 52-week low. In a peer comparison, Colgate-Palmolive (CL), Kimberly-Clark (KMB), Henkel AG (OTCPK:HENOY), Church & Dwight (CHD), and Clorox (CLX) are just as unappealing investments. They all have a “Hold” quant rating.

The grades above suggest that Henkel stock is worth further consideration. Kimberly-Clark has better grades than Colgate-Palmolive. However, its bid to acquire Kenvue (KVUE) for $48.7 billion is unusual. This raises its debt and increases the chances of KMB cutting its dividend.

Your Takeaway

Stock markets will more likely prioritize their bullishness on the ongoing investment growth in artificial intelligence. That would likely diminish the chance of the market declining from a “sell on the news” post-Federal Reserve announcement. The Fed will likely cite job declines in traditional industries as a greater concern than elevated inflation.

Investors should continue to avoid companies in the consumer goods sector. Companies that cannot raise prices to offset inflation and tariffs will report lower profit margins. In this K-shaped economy, investors will likely continue to pivot their portfolios to AI-related names while cutting their exposure to traditional companies sensitive to inflation.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: East West Bancorp: Still Offering Growth At A Reasonable Price
data.description:
2025 is shaping up to be a solid year for California's East West Bancorp (EWBC). Like many banks, balance sheet repricing dynamics have been a nice tailwind for the firm, while East West has delivered strong volume growth as well. All told, that's helped power double-digit earnings growth for the bank this year.

EWBC's shares have also clocked in a good performance. They are up around 16% so far this year, including dividends, outperforming the wider regional bank space by a healthy margin.

I first covered the stock back in the summer, rating it "Buy." Trading for around 1.9x tangible book value, EWBC wasn't an obvious value pick at the time, though companies with above-average profitability and growth rarely look cheap outside of panic periods.

This remains the case today. I won't deny that EWCB could - and probably will - offer better entry points if/when a downturn occurs. But for the long-term investor, today's valuation isn't a barrier to attractive returns.

Strong Volume-Driven Growth

Driven by cheaper funding costs, fixed-asset repricing, and benign credit quality, 2025 has been a better year for bank earnings, with industry-wide net income up around 9% through the first three quarters.

EWBC has done a bit better. The Pasadena-based bank has earned $970 million in adjusted net income so far this year, up 11.5% on the comparable 2024 period. EWBC's adjusted net interest income growth has also outpaced the space, rising just over 10% through the first three quarters versus 5% growth for the industry. NII accounts for over 85% of EWBC's revenue, so trends here are critical to its overall financial performance.

One thing to appreciate about East West is that its funding position isn't particularly strong. Products like Time deposits make up a relatively large share of its liabilities, meaning its interest costs aren't particularly cheap. In a way, this is actually a good thing during easing cycles, because having a more market-like funding base means EWBC can pass on a relatively large share of any interest rate cuts. This is precisely what it has been doing, as shown below.

Like the industry, EWBC has gained more from this process than it has lost on its assets. Indeed, while its blended asset yield fell 28bps through the first three quarters, its cost of funding said earning assets has fallen by more than this. As a result, it delivered around 7bps of adjusted NIM expansion through the first three quarters.

On top of this, EWBC has also been delivering strong volume growth. Average earning asset balances totaled $74.3 billion through the first three quarters, up nearly 8% year-on-year. In fact, this has actually driven most of EWBC's NII growth this year.

This lower reliance on NIM expansion positions EWBC nicely going forward. Cheaper funding and fixed-asset repricing will drive incrementally less benefit from the industry going forward, so volume growth will become more important going forward. Given EWBC hasn't been lacking in this department, I would say that sets it up nicely versus the space.

Credit Holding Up Nicely, Capital Is A Plus

EWBC's $55 billion loan book skewed around 30% to commercial lending at the last count, so there's no denying that the bank is sensitive to the underlying health of the economy. In addition, I would also point out that EWBC has a slightly higher loan concentration than the average bank does (loans were around 72% of its assets last quarter), so its profitability is more geared to developments on the credit front.

That said, trends here remain solid. Despite a resurfacing of credit fears in recent weeks, EWBC's net charge-off rate was just 12bps through the first nine months (13bps last quarter), much lower than the 62bps industry-wide level.

Other metrics aren't really indicating stress, either. Only 0.25% of EWBC's assets were non-performing at the end of last quarter - a ratio that hasn't moved much recently. Delinquencies also look modest. 0.25% of EWBC's loans were between 30 and 89 days past due as of last quarter. While that's higher than at the start of the year, it's still much better than the industry-wide rate (0.54% per the FDIC).

Of course, all of this could change if the economy heads south, and this could force EWBC to raise more provisions. The bank is well positioned if this does occur. I say that for two reasons. First, EWBC is earning a very healthy level of pre-provision income. This line clocked in at $1.39 billion over the first three quarters, for an annualized rate of 3.4% relative to the size of its loan book. In other words, EWBC can absorb quite a lot of losses before it becomes loss-making.

EWBC's balance sheet is also a plus point, with the bank reporting a 14.8% ratio of CET1 capital to risk-weighted assets last quarter. That is more than double its effective regulatory minimum.

Still Offering Growth At A Reasonable Price

EWBC trades for $108.63 as I type, mapping to 1.84x its tangible book value ($58.97 per share as of last quarter). As you can see, this multiple is more or less in line with its ten-year average.

This is what I mean by EWBC not being an obvious value pick. While the lack of a discount to its long-run average would appear to suggest that the shares aren't cheap, note that this period has seen EWBC deliver a ~230% total return (~12.5% annualized), driven by a similar rate of balance sheet growth.

The reason EWBC has delivered this performance is that it has typically earned a 17% return on its tangible equity. Because its dividend consumes just 25% of its net income, it can use most of its earnings to fund a strong rate of growth.

Between balance sheet growth and its current dividend yield (~2.2%), EWBC can deliver a clear double-digit annualized return from its business - and this should be enough to offset any long-term compression to its P/TBV ratio. While its shares could, of course, get cheaper in a downturn, EWBC is still offering today's investor growth at a reasonable price. Accordingly, I am leaving my "Buy" rating in place.

data.title: Janus Henderson Mid Cap Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio management

Justin Tugman, CFA and Kevin Preloger

Investment environment

  • Equity markets rose over the quarter, on positive corporate earnings, increased expectations for Federal Reserve (Fed) rate cuts, and artificial intelligence (AI) enthusiasm. The Fed lowered its target rate by 25 basis points in September, with the market expecting more rate cuts by year-end.
  • A “risk-on,” lower-quality market rally during the quarter resulted in numerous all-time highs achieved. This rally was led by high-beta, lower return-on-equity, and money-losing stocks.

Portfolio review

While our focus on high-quality value companies hindered relative performance in a high-volatility quarter, we remain committed to owning high-quality companies at reasonable valuations, a discipline we believe is especially important given current market valuations and the narrowness of the market performance.

Kirby (KEX), a transporter of petroleum and petrochemical products, hindered relative performance. While short-term demand and utilization have been weak for Kirby, we believe we will see pricing rebound along with demand over the long term, given a lack of industry investment in new capacity.

We had two notable detractors in the consumer discretionary sector. LKQ (LKQ), which sells after-market automotive parts, weighed on relative returns due to soft demand from collision repair customers. It is likely that some lower-end consumers are choosing to pocket insurance proceeds rather than pay to repair their damaged autos, and this has been a headwind for LKQ. Despite weakness in the stock price, we are not adding to our position until we gain clarity regarding both cyclical and secular headwinds facing LKQ.

Shares of retailer Bath & Body Works (BBWI) declined during the period after the company’s new management team issued cautious guidance while indicating that it may take longer than previously expected to achieve sustained revenue growth. While the company continues to innovate with new products and themes, concerns about the lower price points for its products and the health of the lower-end consumer also pressured the stock. We remain invested as we believe the stock is attractively valued relative to fundamentals.

Teradyne (TER), a supplier of testing equipment for semiconductors, contributed to relative performance, benefiting from rising capital spending on AI and other advanced computing. We remain invested in the stock but have trimmed some on recent strength.

Defense stocks such as L3Harris (LHX) were strong relative performers, supported by accelerating global spending on defense and military equipment. We have maintained a sizeable position in L3Harris but have opportunistically trimmed our weighting given the less attractive reward/risk. BWX Technologies, another defense-exposed holding, also was a top performer on a relative basis. As the sole supplier of nuclear power systems to the U.S. Navy, the company is also pursuing other revenue growth opportunities through technologies that serve data-center power needs and the medical isotope industry. That said, we trimmed our position given the strong share price performance and expanded valuation.

The portfolio ended the quarter overweight in the industrials, materials, financials, and consumer discretionary sectors, while it was underweight in the real estate, utilities, consumer staples, energy, communication services, information technology, and healthcare sectors.

Manager outlook

Our sense is that many of the economic crosscurrents that the market has dealt with recently will likely continue for the foreseeable future. The lower-end consumer is strained and is unlikely to experience meaningful relief in the short term, given above-average inflation and a softening labor market. Offsetting this, the middle- to higher-end consumer is on relatively solid footing due to the wealth effect from hard assets and the appreciation of the stock market. Beyond the consumer, continued softness in transportation, usually a sign of a troubled economy, is being offset by the boom in AI- and data center-related spending. Additionally, there is increased clarity regarding tariffs, the potential for the Fed to continue cutting rates, and tax-related stimulus that may kick in as we progress toward the end of the year. Thus, we may continue to see gains despite signs of froth in the market.

While volatility has been negligible thus far, it may increase given recent market imbalances. Excitement around AI has been a strong driver of market returns, and we have benefited from some of this excitement. But this has driven some stock valuations to elevated levels that may be unsupported by fundamentals. The focus on AI has also led investors to overlook compelling ideas in other parts of the market, providing a potentially good opportunity for stock selection, especially in the small- and mid-capitalization ranges. As always, we remain committed to our focus on quality as we seek out well-managed, profitable companies with low debt levels that we believe are undervalued by the market. We believe such companies have potential to outperform regardless of the economic backdrop.

Definitions

Volatility measures risk using the dispersion of returns for a given investment.

Basis point (bp) equals 1/100 of a percentage point. 1 bp = 0.01%, 100 bps = 1%.

Beta measures the volatility of a security or portfolio relative to an index. Less than one means lower volatility than the index; more than one means greater volatility.

Monetary Policy refers to the policies of a central bank, aimed at influencing the level of inflation and growth in an economy. It includes controlling interest rates and the supply of money.

Please consider the charges, risks, expenses and investment objectives carefully before investing. For a prospectus or, if available, a summary prospectus containing this and other information, please call Janus Henderson at 800.668.0434 or download the file from janushenderson.com/info. Read it carefully before you invest or send money.

Returns include reinvestment of dividends and capital gains. Discussion is based on the performance of Class I Shares.

This Fund has a performance-based management fee that may adjust up or down based on the Fund’s performance. Closed to certain new investors.

The opinions are as of 09/30/25, are subject to change and may not reflect the views of others in the organization. Janus Henderson may have a business relationship with certain entities discussed. The comments should not be construed as a recommendation of individual holdings or market sectors, but as an illustration of broader themes.

Mutual fund performance for some periods may incorporate returns from older share classes, adjusted for fees and expenses. Visit janushenderson.com/synthetic for specific details. Holdings are subject to change without notice.

For equity portfolios, relative contribution compares the performance of a security in the portfolio to the benchmark’s total return, factoring in the difference in weight of that security in the benchmark. Returns are calculated using daily returns and previous day ending weights rolled up by ticker, gross of advisory fees, may exclude certain derivatives and does not represent actual performance.

There is no assurance the stated objective(s) will be met.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal and fluctuation of value.

Real estate securities, including Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs), are sensitive to changes in real estate values and rental income, property taxes, interest rates, tax and regulatory requirements, supply and demand, and the management skill and creditworthiness of the company. Additionally REITs could fail to qualify for certain tax-benefits or registration exemptions which could produce adverse economic consequences.

Equity securities are subject to risks including market risk. Returns will fluctuate in response to issuer, political and economic developments.

Value stocks can continue to be undervalued by the market for long periods of time and may not appreciate to the extent expected.

Initial Public Offerings (IPOs) are highly speculative investments and may be subject to lower liquidity and greater volatility. Special risks associated with IPOs include limited operating history, unseasoned trading, high turnover and non-repeatable performance.

ESG Integration Risk. There is a risk that considering ESG Factors as part of the Fund’s investment process may fail to produce the intended results or that the Fund may perform differently from funds that have a similar investment style but do not formally incorporate such considerations in their strategy. Information related to ESG Factors provided by issuers and third parties, which portfolio management may utilize, continues to develop, and may be incomplete or inaccurate, use different methodologies, or be applied differently across issuers and industries.

ESG Integration. As part of its investment process, portfolio management considers ESG risks and opportunities (“ESG Factors”) that it believes are financially material, alongside other fundamental investment factors. Examples of potential financially material ESG Factors include corporate governance, company culture, exposure to climate change, and human capital management. To assess ESG Factors, portfolio management uses issuer reports, third-party data, and internally-generated analyses and may engage directly with issuers. ESG Factors are one of many considerations in the investment decision-making process and may not be determinative in deciding to include or exclude an investment from the portfolio.

Smaller capitalization securities may be less stable and more susceptible to adverse developments, and may be more volatile and less liquid than larger capitalization securities. Actively managed investment portfolios are subject to the risk that the investment strategies and research process employed may fail to produce the intended results. Accordingly, a portfolio may underperform its benchmark index or other investment products with similar investment objectives.

Russell Midcap® Value Index reflects the performance of U.S. mid-cap equities with lower price-to-book ratios and lower forecasted growth values. Index performance does not reflect the expenses of managing a portfolio as an index is unmanaged and not available for direct investment.

Janus Henderson is a trademark of Janus Henderson Group plc or one of its subsidiaries. © Janus Henderson Group plc. Mutual funds distributed by Janus Henderson Distributors US LLC

data.title: Treasury Issuance Is Creating A Liquidity Storm Beneath The Market's Surface
data.description:
The past week suggests that liquidity constraints persist and will, at some point, force the Fed to begin increasing the size of the balance sheet. But for now, those constraints show up in both overnight funding rates remaining elevated and in primary dealers' balance sheets, which are seeing significant rises in Treasury holdings.

These are all signs that suggest the recent deluge of Treasury debt has drained reserves and clogged primary dealer balance sheets. Additionally, risky assets like Bitcoin have collapsed, and the stock market has seen volatility increase while trading has been basically sideways.

Reserves Remain Low

Reserve balances at the Fed remain basically unchanged this past week, even now that quantitative tightening has completed. A sign that reserve balances are likely to stay below the $3 trillion mark for the foreseeable future or until such a time that the Fed decides to expand the balance sheet actively. Reserves will continue to ebb and flow with changes in the Treasury General Account or other liability factors, but, in total, they are unlikely to deviate much.

What is also notable is that liquidity constraints are not confined to the reserves held at the Fed. Now that the TGA is filled, reserves are no longer being drained. The pressures from debt issuance are now building on the balance sheets of primary dealers. Recently, there has been a surge in primary dealers' holdings of Treasury bills and, more generally, Treasuries. In fact, Treasury holdings have reached their highest level ever.

Together, this suggests that liquidity constraints continue to build in the market, which is why we continue to see the secured overnight financing rate trade above the Fed's effective funds rate and, more importantly, big swings and increased volatility in the overnight funding market rates.

Impacts Are Being Felt

The tightening of liquidity conditions has apparently and predictably had a meaningful impact on risk assets like Bitcoin, which is down about 29% from its October peak, with its movement loosely reflecting changes in reserve balances over time.

The equity market has been trading in a wide range, as it seems to be moving around in much broader ranges more recently, which is not surprising given that liquidity in the overnight market has tightened. In fact, in 2018, when the balance sheet shrank, the index traded sideways from January 2018 until October 2019, with just a lot of volatility in between.

During that time frame, in 2018, we saw a similar type of fluctuation between the secured overnight financing rate and the effective funds rate. It wasn't until the overnight funding rate spike in October 2019 that the Fed was forced to end QT and restart balance sheet expansion.

Arguably, the Fed has tools in place to prevent a similar occurrence to the 2019 repo rate spike, with the standing repo facility in place. But what it underscores is that market dynamics today are comparable to those back in 2018 and 2019, when the reserve balance got too low.

So while it may seem that the effects of tighter liquidity are not being felt in risk assets, the decline in Bitcoin suggests they are. Additionally, the increased volatility and sideways trading in the equity market also suggest they are being felt as well.

It would seem they may only begin to feel them when primary dealers are forced to absorb increased Treasury issuance now that the TGA has been replenished, and those impacts are likely yet to be felt.
